![JD Supra](/img/logo/logo-jdsupra-opt.svg)

# SEC Set the Stage for Potential ESG Reporting Requirements

![Akin Gump Strauss Hauer & Feld LLP](/img/client_headers/AkinGump/Header.jpg)

![Akin Gump Strauss Hauer & Feld LLP](/img/client_headers/AkinGump/Header.jpg)

On February 24, 2021, Acting Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) Chair Allison Herren Lee issued a [statement](https://www.sec.gov/news/public-statement/lee-statement-review-climate-related-disclosure) directing the Division of Corporation Finance to “enhance its focus on climate-related disclosure in public company filings.” Accordingly, Acting Chair Lee announced that, after completing a review of public companies’ efforts and compliance in regards to the SEC’s 2010 interpretative [guidance](https://www.sec.gov/rules/interp/2010/33-9106.pdf) to public companies regarding existing SEC disclosure requirements as they apply to climate change matters (2010 guidance), the SEC staff will update the 2010 guidance to reflect developments in the past decade. The staff’s review will include engagement with public companies and absorption of “critical lessons on how the market is currently managing climate-related risks.” She closed her statement by noting that, “[n]ow more than ever, investors are considering climate-related issues when making their investment decisions” and that ensuring compliance with current rules and updating existing guidelines are steps the SEC can take to produce “consistent, comparable, and reliable climate-related disclosures.”

The 2010 guidance summarized a number of SEC rules and regulations with the potential to be a source of disclosure obligations for public companies regarding climate change, including:

When determining what climate-related disclosures they may have to make, the 2010 guidance urged companies to consider:

While it is not yet apparent what changes will be made to the 2010 guidance or existing regulations, Lee’s statement suggests that she considers updated guidance to be an “immediate”—and perhaps just the first—step to eventual climate and other environmental, social and corporate governance (ESG) disclosure requirements. Indeed, President Biden [promised](https://joebiden.com/climate-plan/) during his campaign to require public reporting companies to “disclose climate risk and the greenhouse gas emissions in their operations and supply chains.” Mandatory ESG reporting requirements may therefore be on the agenda for President Biden’s SEC chair nominee Gary Gensler, whose nomination we discussed further [here](https://www.akingump.com/en/news-insights/bidens-money-cop-to-shine-a-light-on-esg-disclosure-as-sec-requirementsand-a-potential-universal-reporting-frameworkappear-imminent.html), and the SEC’s new Senior Policy Advisor for Climate and ESG, Satyam Khanna, who [we expect](https://www.akingump.com/en/experience/industries/energy/speaking-energy/theres-a-new-esg-sheriff-in-town-at-the-sec.html) to work closely with Gensler, Lee and others at the SEC to implement President Biden’s promise. Accordingly, companies should remain vigilant for changes to their disclosure requirements as the SEC enhances its focus on climate-related issues. In the meantime, directors and officers may consider taking a step back to assess where their companies stand with regard to ESG issues and disclosure and to prepare for a future likely to bring greater scrutiny.

For greater detail on ESG issues that boards of directors should consider at this time, please see Akin Gump’s [Top 10 Topics for Directors in 2021](https://www.akingump.com/a/web/mn3uYifw1YruYKH5fKH2qR/top10_directors_020921-9.pdf).

The Global ESG Reporting Movement: A Historical Overview
========================================================

October 20, 2023[by Team IRIS CARBON](https://iriscarbon.com/author/team-iris-carbon/)[0](https://iriscarbon.com/the-global-esg-reporting-movement-a-historical-overview/#comments)

**Table of Contents**[hide](https://iriscarbon.com/the-global-esg-reporting-movement-a-historical-overview/#)

[The Birth of ESG Reporting](https://iriscarbon.com/the-global-esg-reporting-movement-a-historical-overview/#The_Birth_of_ESG_Reporting)

[The Emergence of Social Responsibility](https://iriscarbon.com/the-global-esg-reporting-movement-a-historical-overview/#The_Emergence_of_Social_Responsibility)

[The Birth of Modern ESG Reporting](https://iriscarbon.com/the-global-esg-reporting-movement-a-historical-overview/#The_Birth_of_Modern_ESG_Reporting)

[ESG Reporting goes Mainstream](https://iriscarbon.com/the-global-esg-reporting-movement-a-historical-overview/#ESG_Reporting_goes_Mainstream)

[The 2010s: ESG Reporting Goes GlobaL](https://iriscarbon.com/the-global-esg-reporting-movement-a-historical-overview/#The_2010s_ESG_Reporting_Goes_GlobaL)

[The Present and Future of ESG Reporting](https://iriscarbon.com/the-global-esg-reporting-movement-a-historical-overview/#The_Present_and_Future_of_ESG_Reporting)

[Key trends and future developments include:](https://iriscarbon.com/the-global-esg-reporting-movement-a-historical-overview/#Key_trends_and_future_developments_include)

[Current Status](https://iriscarbon.com/the-global-esg-reporting-movement-a-historical-overview/#Current_Status)

[Conclusion](https://iriscarbon.com/the-global-esg-reporting-movement-a-historical-overview/#Conclusion)

### Introduction

In recent years, there has been a significant shift in the way companies report their performance to stakeholders. [Environmental, Social, and Governance (ESG) reporting](https://iriscarbon.com/products/iris-carbon-for-esg-reporting/) has become a critical tool for businesses worldwide, reflecting a growing awareness of the interconnectedness of corporate success and societal well-being.

ESG is continually becoming a greater factor in a company’s success in attracting, engaging and retaining employees correlating to the demographics of the current workforce. Millennials are now the major demographic representing 34% of today’s workforce, while generation Z consists of 21%. These demographic groups especially value the issues addressed by ESG.

This blog takes a deep dive into the historical evolution of the global ESG reporting movement, tracing its origins, milestones, and its current status as a vital component of modern business practices.

### The Birth of ESG Reporting

**Environmental Awareness:**The early 1970s marked the beginning of environmental consciousness. With growing concerns about pollution, resource depletion, and other environmental issues, companies felt the need to disclose their environmental impact.

**Regulatory Initiatives:** Legislation such as the [National Environmental Policy Act](https://www.epa.gov/nepa/what-national-environmental-policy-act#:~:text=The%20National%20Environmental%20Policy%20Act%20(NEPA)%20was%20signed%20into%20law,actions%20prior%20to%20making%20decisions.) in the United States played a pivotal role in compelling companies to report on their environmental activities and impacts. This marked the first instance of mandatory environmental reporting.

### The Emergence of Social Responsibility

[**Socially responsible investing**](https://www.investopedia.com/terms/s/sri.asp)**(SRI)**: The 1980s saw the rise of SRI, where investors began to consider not only financial returns but also the social and ethical behavior of the companies in which they invested. This shift in investor priorities started to influence corporate behavior and reporting.

**South Africa Sullivan Principles:** The Sullivan Principles were established as a corporate code of conduct addressing apartheid in South Africa. They highlighted the importance of businesses considering social issues and the need for transparency in reporting on such matters.

### The Birth of Modern ESG Reporting

The 1990s saw the emergence of the modern ESG reporting movement. Key milestones during this period included:

1.   **The**[**Global Reporting Initiative**](https://www.investopedia.com/global-reporting-initiative-7483127)**(GRI):** Founded in 1997, GRI became one of the most recognized frameworks for sustainability reporting. GRI’s guidelines provided companies with a structured way to report on their economic, environmental, and social impacts. Today, it is widely adopted by companies globally.
2.   **The United Nations Global Compact:**Launched in 1999, this initiative encouraged businesses to commit to ten principles covering human rights, labor, environment, and anti-corruption. Companies were expected to provide annual reports detailing their progress, further promoting ESG reporting.

### ESG Reporting goes Mainstream

The 2000s witnessed a surge in the adoption of ESG reporting as companies recognized its significance for reputation, risk management, and long-term sustainability. Key developments during this period included:

1.   **Integration into Financial Reporting:** ESG factors began to be recognized as material for financial performance. In 2009, the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) issued guidance on disclosing climate change-related risks, pushing ESG considerations into mainstream financial reporting.
2.   **Standardization Efforts:** Organizations like the International Integrated Reporting Council (IIRC) and the Sustainability Accounting Standards Board (SASB) emerged to promote uniform standards for ESG reporting, enhancing comparability between companies.

### The 2010s: ESG Reporting Goes GlobaL

The 2010s saw ESG reporting expand beyond traditional Western markets. Some key developments in this decade include:

1.   **Paris Agreement and the UN Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs):** The [Paris Agreement](https://unfccc.int/process-and-meetings/the-paris-agreement) in 2015 and the UN’s 17 Sustainable Development Goals provided a global framework for environmental and social reporting. Companies worldwide began aligning their efforts with these goals.
2.   **Mandatory Reporting:** Several countries introduced mandatory ESG reporting requirements. For example, the European Union mandated non-financial reporting for large companies in 2017, and China introduced ESG disclosure guidelines in 2019.

### The Present and Future of ESG Reporting

Today, ESG reporting has become a fundamental part of corporate strategy. Investors, consumers, and regulators demand greater transparency, forcing companies to take ESG seriously. The task ahead involves increasing standardization, improving data quality, and integrating ESG considerations even further into financial reporting.

### Key trends and future developments include:

**1.Technology and Data:** Advancements in technology and data analytics have significantly impacted ESG reporting, making it more efficient, accurate, and comprehensive.

*   **Data Analytics:** The use of data analytics tools allows companies to process large volumes of data, identify trends, and measure ESG performance more effectively. For example, tools like Tableau and Power BI are widely used for data visualization and analysis.
*   **Artificial Intelligence (AI):** AI and machine learning algorithms are being employed to automate data collection and analysis, helping companies to identify emerging ESG risks and opportunities. ESG data providers like Truvalue Labs use AI to assess company ESG performance.
*   **Blockchain:** Blockchain technology enhances the transparency and security of ESG data. Companies can use blockchain to create immutable records of their sustainability efforts, providing a higher level of trust.
*   **Software Solutions:**ESG reporting software frequently incorporates benchmarking functionalities. Notably, solutions such as [IRIS CARBON®](https://iriscarbon.com/products/iris-carbon-for-esg-reporting/) supports ESG report authoring and enables digital transformation of sustainability data globally using XBRL format, as the first SaaS solution to do so.

**2. Stakeholder Engagement:**Effective engagement with stakeholders is a critical aspect of ESG reporting. Companies are increasingly recognizing the importance of involving various groups in their ESG strategies.

*   **Employees:** Engaging employees in ESG initiatives can lead to a more committed workforce. Companies like Patagonia involve employees in environmental and social programs, aligning them with the company’s values.
*   **Customers:**Engaging with customers allows companies to respond to their concerns and preferences, demonstrating a commitment to responsible business practices. Sustainable brands like Tesla actively engage with their customer base.
*   **Local Communities:**Collaborating with local communities helps companies understand and mitigate potential ESG risks, build trust, and contribute positively to the areas in which they operate. Companies like Unilever engage with local communities to support sustainable sourcing of raw materials.

**3. Regulatory Evolution:**The regulatory landscape for ESG reporting is continually evolving. Governments and international bodies are taking steps to standardize and mandate ESG disclosure.

**European Union (EU) Directive:**The EU has introduced mandatory ESG reporting requirements for large companies. The [Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive](https://iriscarbon.com/the-eu-corporate-sustainability-reporting-directive-csrd-new-rules-you-need-to-know/) (CSRD) is a notable example of this effort.

**Sustainability Standards and Reporting Bodies:**Various organizations are working on establishing global sustainability standards. [The International Sustainability Standards Board (ISSB)](https://iriscarbon.com/latest-developments-at-the-international-sustainability-standards-board-issb/), having absorbed SASB and IIRC, is now the key driver in advancing the convergence of ESG standards on a global level.

**UN Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs)**: The [UN’s 17 Sustainable Development Goals](https://sdgs.un.org/goals) are gaining recognition and can potentially lead to more standardized reporting on ESG issues globally.

These trends and future developments in ESG reporting reflect the growing importance of transparency, accountability, and sustainability in modern business practices. As technology evolves, stakeholders become more involved, and regulations become more stringent, companies will continue to adapt and expand their ESG reporting efforts to meet the demands of the changing business landscape.

### Current Status

According to [Bloomberg data from 2023](https://www.bloomberg.com/professional/blog/esg-assets-may-hit-53-trillion-by-2025-a-third-of-global-aum/), the total global investment in ESG funds has reached over $30 Trillion. Investors jumped on board quickly. Governments, investors, and other stakeholders have established several initiatives, including the [Global Reporting Initiative](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Global_Reporting_Initiative) (GRI), International Sustainability Standards Board (ISSB), with specific guidelines to ensure that businesses can produce sustainability reports that effectively meet these expectations.

To develop effective corporate sustainability strategies, it is necessary to both identify areas for improvement and to take advantage of an organization’s operational strengths, such as its use of renewable energy sources or the development of cutting-edge products with low carbon footprints. Because of the improved brand reputation among environmentally sensitive consumers, this can assist in lower waste management costs while also enhancing customer loyalty.

Organizations can better guarantee that their sustainability initiatives are in line with international standards by knowing the requirements for [ESG reporting.](https://iriscarbon.com/products/iris-carbon-for-esg-reporting/) Understanding the value of corporate sustainability and how it affects overall performance is the next step.

### Conclusion

The global ESG reporting movement has come a long way since its inception in the 1970s. It has evolved from a niche concept to a mainstream practice, underlining the integral role that ESG factors play in shaping a company’s reputation, risk management, and long-term viability.

In conclusion, the evolving ESG landscape holds critical implications for both businesses and ESG-focused investors. As the demand for high-quality ESG data grows, it’s essential to establish robust data management processes now. This changing landscape offers businesses the opportunity to enhance transparency and demonstrate leadership. As businesses and regulators worldwide continue to recognize the importance of ESG, this movement will likely be instrumental in driving sustainable business practices and contributing to a better future for all.

# Understanding the SECâs climate risk disclosure rule

More about the new proposed climate risk disclosure ruleâhow it impacts companies, investors, and the environment.

![large grey building with geometric shapes](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/62733f1361e2ad64f8790984/6419c4a4f2cf266e13cdbe4e_Sustain.Life-understanding-sec-climate-risk-disclosure-rule.jpg)
![Fact sheet: SEC climate disclosure rule cover](https://no-cache.hubspot.com/cta/default/9205592/interactive-164169181987.png)

â

### UPDATE

On March 6, 2024, the Securities and Exchange Commission adopted final rules to require registrants to disclose certain climate-related information in registration statements and annual reports. [Learn how your company will be impacted](https://2022-q2-marketing.webflow.io/blog/video-sec-new-climate-disclosure-rule-passes).

â

On January 4, 2023, the U.S. SEC announced its FallÂ [2023 SEC regulatory agenda](http://www.sustain.life/blog/sec-regulatory-agenda-2023). While there were more than 24 rules set to be finalized in the spring and fall, one rule, in particular, has garnered a significant amount of attention: the SEC climate disclosure rule.Â 

If adopted, this amendment to a 2010 Guidance on ESG Disclosures rule would require publicly traded companies to enhance and standardize the disclosure of the companyâs exposure to climate change risks and the potential impacts such exposure may have on the business and its future plans.Â 

But what exactly does the SEC climate disclosure rule cover and entail?

Read this overview to learn more about its background, scope,Â [corporate sustainability reportingÂ](https://www.sustain.life/report)requirements, consequences of noncompliance, as well as implications for companies and investors.Â Â 

â

### In this article, weâll cover

[Background and scope of the SEC climate disclosures](#Background)

[Reporting requirements](#Requirements)

[Consequences of noncompliance](#Consequences)

[Benefits of the rule for companies and investors](#Benefits)

[Challenges and costs associated with compliance](#Challenges)

[Implications For companies and investors](#Implications)

â

## Background and scope of the SEC climate disclosuresÂ Â

In 2010, the SEC issuedÂ [release 33-9106](https://www.sec.gov/comments/climate-disclosure/cll12-8804071-237954.pdf), Guidance on Climate Change Disclosures, which encouraged companies to inform investors about material risks, uncertainties, impacts, and opportunities as they relate to climate change. The SEC wanted registrants to:

The purpose of this was to provide investors with clear and comprehensive ESG data and metrics about how climate risks affected investment strategy and financial performance.Â 

While this rule paved the way for future disclosure rules, it was largely toothless, lacking specificity, broad-scale applicability, and enforcement mechanisms. For most companies, disclosure was done voluntarily. And, asÂ [Sara Dewey notes](https://eelp.law.harvard.edu/2022/04/what-to-know-about-the-sec-proposed-climate-risk-disclosure-rule/)Â in Harvardâs Environmental & Energy Law Program, this information was often inconsistent and fragmented:Â 

â70 percent of companies in the Russell 1000 published sustainability reports in 2020, yet 86 percent of institutional investors surveyed in 2021 were skeptical about companiesâ ability to deliver on their sustainability goals. Investors are calling for standardizedÂ [integrated reporting](http://www.sustain.life/blog/what-is-integrated-reporting)Â requirements to enhance the comparability, reliability, and transparency of this information.âÂ 

To address such flaws, the SECâs new climate risk disclosure rule sought to enhance the original rule by making disclosures âconsistent, comparable, and reliable.â It enhanced the existing rules by pulling from both theÂ [Task Force on Climate-Related Financial Disclosure](https://www.fsb-tcfd.org/)Â (TCFD) as well as theÂ [GHG protocol](https://ghgprotocol.org/), which introduced the concepts of âscopes of emissions.âÂ

### Overview of the SECâs climate assessment and reporting requirements

The proposed SEC climate disclosure is lengthy, spanning more than 500 pages. Needless to say, thereâs a lot to unpack.Â 

Generally speaking, it contains guidance recommendations that would compel companies to report on their climate risks and opportunities annually. Within that report, they must detail their industry-specific climate-related risks and opportunities, including the methods used to assess and manage them.Â 

The new rule can be broken down into three categories of disclosure:Â 

**1. Material climate impactsÂ**â Companies are expected to disclose:

**2. Greenhouse gas emissions**Â â Companies are required to report auditedÂ [scope 1](https://www.sustain.life/blog/scope-1-emissions)Â andÂ [scope 2 emissions](https://www.sustain.life/blog/scope-2-emissions), which are directly generated by the company through its operations or through the energy it buys. In some cases, filers must also includeÂ [scope 3 disclosures](https://www.sustain.life/blog/scope-3-emissions)Â if theyâre material or if theyâre relevant to the filerâs targeted goals.Â **â**

**3. Targets and transition plans**Â â Companies are expected to disclose any existing targets regarding energy usage, emissions reductions, conservation, etc., as well as plans for achieving those targets.Â

### SEC climate risk disclosure ruleâs scope, applicability, and timeframeÂ

Publicly traded companies have been put on notice. As of now, April 2023 is the release date for this final climate-related disclosure rule. If approved, the phase-in period would likely become effective in April 2024. However, itâs important to note that smallerâread: non-publicâreporting companies would likely be exempt from scope 3 GHG emissions disclosures and have an additional year to transition.Â

That said, it remains to be seen what impact the Supreme Courtâs recent ruling inÂ [W. Virginia v. EPA](https://www.supremecourt.gov/opinions/21pdf/20-1530_n758.pdf)âwhich determined that the EPA has no constitutional authority to regulate carbon emissionsâwill have on SECsâ ability to regulate climate-related risk disclosures. In all likelihood, a new spate of legal challenges will soon call into question the SECâs rule-making authority.Â  Â

Even so, the writing is on the wallâstronger climate-related regulations are coming to the States soon. Knowing this, proactive companiesâincluding private entitiesâare making necessary changes to address sustainability and disclosures sooner rather than later by:Â 

## Reporting requirementsÂ

Naturally, many companies are currently scratching their heads, wondering whether the disclosure rule will apply to them and, if so, when theyâll need to act.Â 

### Who does the rule apply to?Â

This proposed rule would apply to U.S. 10-K filers as well as foreign private issuers who file 20-F forms with the SEC.

### What Is the reporting timeframe?Â

As mentioned, this depends on if and when the law is passed and whether there is a legal challenge to the SECâs authority to regulate such activities.Â 

If it does pass, large companies would have to disclose the information for fiscal year 2024, so filing year 2025. Smaller companies would have an additional year-long grace period until fiscal year 2025.Â Â 

All filers would also be granted an additional year beyond those deadlines to report scope 3 emissions.  
â

|  | All proposed SEC climate disclosure metrics, including scope 1 and 2 emisisons, excluding scope 3 | Scope 3 disclosures |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **Large accelerated filers (market cap. over $700 million)** | Fiscal year 2023 (files in 2024) | Fiscal year 2024 (files in 2025) |
| **Accelerated filers, non-accelerated filers** | Fiscal year 2024 (files in 2025) | Fiscal year 2025 (files in 2026) |
| **Small companies** | Fiscal year 2025 (files in 2026) | Exempt |

### What types of information must be disclosed?Â

There are dozens of potential disclosures that a company may be required to reveal. That said, some of the more broad disclosures include:Â 

Additionally, companies are required to report GHG emissions, including:Â

## Consequences of noncomplianceÂ

As of now, the SEC rules have not yet proposed penalties for noncompliance, though itâs reasonable to assume that failure to comply could result in significant consequences, including reputational harm, legal liability, and financial penalties.Â 

Last year, Californiaâs Climate Disclosure legislationÂ [adopted amendments](https://www.akingump.com/en/experience/practices/climate-change/speaking-sustainability/california-climate-disclosure-legislation-clears-critical-hurdles.html)Â that set administrative and civil penalties if a reporting entity failed to comply with requirements:Â Â 

## Benefits of the rule for companies and investors

Both companies and investors stand to benefit from these climate-related disclosure rule changes.Â Â 

### For companiesÂ

By complying with these rules, a company will be better positioned to meet customer and stakeholder expectations thanks to:Â 

### For investorsÂ

These disclosures create transparency and visibility for investors that enable them to:

## Challenges and costs associated with compliance

Naturally, complying with these new regulations can present operational challenges for publicly traded companies.

This is a large and complex regulatory bill. Simply understanding what is expected of a company will be a significant challenge in and of itself, let alone making the necessary changes to comply with the rules.Â 

The largest operational challenge and cost will likely involve locating and collating all of the relevant emissions data. For that, having robust sustainability software makes it much easier to measure your environmental data, build a plan that reduces your carbon emissions, and then report your actions in a manner thatâs compliant with the SECâs standards.Â Â 

## Implications For companies and investorsÂ

While publicly traded companies may be the focus of this rule, chances are, it will have significant downstream effects.

For example, to report scope 3 emissions, public companies will very likely need to request their suppliersâ emissions data. As a result, both companies large and small, will need to take the necessary steps to create visibility and control over their organizational emissions data.Â Â 

## Complying with the SECâs climate risk disclosure ruleÂ

While the SECâs newest climate risk disclosure rule hasnât yet been set in stone, all signs indicate that either this specific rule orÂ *a*Â rule like it will soon become the national standard.Â 

By taking proactive steps now to measure and manage your greenhouse gas emissions data, youâll be better prepared for any future disclosure laws.Â 

Wondering where to start?Â 

Sustain. LifeâsÂ [sustainability management software](https://www.sustain.life/)Â provides advanced carbon emissions measurement and decarbonization capabilities for leading companies that need to take meaningful climate action.Â

# SEC Set the Stage for Potential ESG Reporting Requirements

Feb 25, 2021

![](https://www.akingump.com/a/web/sJ9oFGaL5jz7vuM5gGcjAG/aa4QF9/speaking-sustainability_980x574.png)

By: [Kerry E. Berchem](https://www.akingump.com/en/lawyers-advisors/kerry-e-berchem)

The 2010 guidance summarized a number of SEC rules and regulations with the potential to be a source of disclosure obligations for public companies regarding climate change, including:

When determining what climate-related disclosures they may have to make, the 2010 guidance urged companies to consider:

While it is not yet apparent what changes will be made to the 2010 guidance or existing regulations, Lee’s statement suggests that she considers updated guidance to be an “immediate”—and perhaps just the first—step to eventual climate and other environmental, social and corporate governance (ESG) disclosure requirements. Indeed, President Biden [promised](https://joebiden.com/climate-plan/) during his campaign to require public reporting companies to “disclose climate risk and the greenhouse gas emissions in their operations and supply chains.” Mandatory ESG reporting requirements may therefore be on the agenda for President Biden’s SEC chair nominee Gary Gensler, whose nomination we discussed further [here](https://www.akingump.com/en/news-insights/bidens-money-cop-to-shine-a-light-on-esg-disclosure-as-sec-requirementsand-a-potential-universal-reporting-frameworkappear-imminent.html), and the SEC’s new Senior Policy Advisor for Climate and ESG, Satyam Khanna, who [we expect](https://www.akingump.com/en/experience/industries/energy/speaking-energy/theres-a-new-esg-sheriff-in-town-at-the-sec.html) to work closely with Gensler, Lee and others at the SEC to implement President Biden’s promise. Accordingly, companies should remain vigilant for changes to their disclosure requirements as the SEC enhances its focus on climate-related issues. In the meantime, directors and officers may consider taking a step back to assess where their companies stand with regard to ESG issues and disclosure and to prepare for a future likely to bring greater scrutiny.

For greater detail on ESG issues that boards of directors should consider at this time, please see Akin Gump’s [Top 10 Topics for Directors in 2021](https://www.akingump.com/a/web/mn3uYifw1YruYKH5fKH2qR/top10_directors_020921-9.pdf).

June 30, 2025

[The EU Simplifies CBAM – What Are the Potential Implications?](https://www.akingump.com/en/insights/blogs/speaking-sustainability/the-eu-simplifies-cbam-what-are-the-potential-implications)

The European Parliament and Council reached a [**provisional agreement**](https://www.consilium.europa.eu/en/press/press-releases/2025/06/18/carbon-border-adjustment-mechanism-cbam-council-and-parliament-strike-a-deal-on-its-simplification/) (i.e., a post-consultation, non-binding political deal in relation to the final text of a legislative proposal) to streamline the European Union’s (EU) Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism (CBAM) on June 18, 2025. This is a key instrument to prevent carbon leakage and align trade policy with the EU’s climate goals. The changes are part of the EU’s broader sustainability legislative simplification package announced earlier this year. This proposal is intended to ease compliance burdens while maintaining the environmental integrity of the CBAM framework.

[Wind Energy Leasing Moratorium Status Update](https://www.akingump.com/en/insights/blogs/speaking-sustainability/wind-energy-leasing-moratorium-status-update)

Wind energy projects along the coasts are facing uncertainty due to President Trump’s Presidential Memorandum[1](#_ftn1) issued on January 20, “[Temporary Withdrawal of All Areas on the Outer Continental Shelf from Offshore Wind Leasing and Review of the Federal Government’s Leasing and Permitting Practices for Wind Projects](https://www.whitehouse.gov/presidential-actions/2025/01/temporary-withdrawal-of-all-areas-on-the-outer-continental-shelf-from-offshore-wind-leasing-and-review-of-the-federal-governments-leasing-and-permitting-practices-for-wind-projects/).” This Memorandum introduces substantial policy changes that impact both onshore and offshore wind development.

[Read More](/en/insights/blogs/speaking-sustainability/wind-energy-leasing-moratorium-status-update)

Speaking Sustainability

January 24, 2025

[This Week's Climate Policy Update | January 20 - 24, 2025](https://www.akingump.com/en/insights/blogs/speaking-sustainability/this-weeks-climate-policy-update-or-january-20-24-2025)

Beginning on Monday, there have been a flurry of executive orders from the Trump administration reversing Biden-era energy policies, emphasizing oil and gas production, lifting the liquified natural gas (LNG) export permitting pause and withdrawing from all accords and commitments under the United Nations Framework Convention on Climate Change (UNFCCC) including the Paris climate agreement. The orders also target electric vehicles (EVs), wind energy, international climate aid and the use of the social cost of carbon in agency decision making. For close tracking of these orders and more to come, visit the [Akin Trump Executive Order tracker](https://www.akingump.com/en/insights/trump-executive-order-overview). Concurrently, President Trump’s nominees for the Department of the Interior (DOI), Department of Energy (DOE) and Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) have each passed their initial rounds of committee confirmation votes, and now await votes before the Senate floor.

[Read More](/en/insights/blogs/speaking-sustainability/this-weeks-climate-policy-update-or-january-20-24-2025)

Speaking Sustainability

January 10, 2025

[This Week's Climate Policy Update | January 6 - 10, 2025](https://www.akingump.com/en/insights/blogs/speaking-sustainability/this-weeks-climate-policy-update-or-january-6-10-2025)

In the final days of his term, President Joe Biden has taken significant steps to solidify his administration’s climate legacy. The administration finalized rules for various clean energy tax credits established under the Inflation Reduction Act. However, these rules, intended to stimulate clean energy advancements through 2032, face opposition from Congressional Republicans, who are considering scaling back or repealing the credits through budget reconciliation.

[Read More](/en/insights/blogs/speaking-sustainability/this-weeks-climate-policy-update-or-january-6-10-2025)

The twilight hours of the Biden administration and the 118th Congress have been marked by intense legislative and regulatory activity, underscored by President-elect Trump’s derailment of last-minute congressional budget talks, and stalled progress on energy permitting reforms.

enriched_points.expanded_content: The 2010s saw the introduction of mandatory ESG reporting laws, compelling companies to disclose their sustainability practices, enhancing transparency and accountability to stakeholders.
The European Union enacted regulations in 2017, mandating large companies to report non-financial information, setting a precedent for global ESG reporting standards.
Companies began embedding ESG factors into their core strategies, recognizing that sustainable practices drive long-term value and align with stakeholder expectations.
